critic_raw:
## Critique of the Offline Benchmark Design

This is a substantive assessment, not a review of polish. I'll organize by root cause — each group of issues shares an underlying assumption that the design has not verified.

---

### Group A — The Candidate Pool Confound Invalidates the Primary Comparison

**1. The specific claim being made**
The experiment claims the new stack's NDCG@20 improvement reflects superior ranking quality. But NDCG@20 is computed over each system's own candidate pool — 500 items for the new stack, 200 for the legacy. The reranker is not being tested on the same task. It is being tested on a different input distribution with a different recall ceiling.

**Why that claim might be wrong**
NDCG@20 asks: given the candidates I was handed, how well did I rank the top 20? A reranker handed 500 candidates will, in expectation, receive more relevant items simply because the retriever has more surface area. Even a *weaker* reranker applied to the 500-item pool may outscore a stronger reranker applied to the 200-item pool if the retrieval recall improvement is large enough. The 4.5% relative gain is a compound quantity — it absorbs both the retrieval recall lift and any ranking signal change — but the experiment has no instrument to separate them.

**Evidence required**
Cross-evaluate both rerankers on both candidate pools in a 2×2 factorial:

|  | Legacy 200-item pool | New 500-item pool |
|---|---|---|
| **Legacy reranker** | Legacy NDCG@20 (baseline) | Retrieval-only lift |
| **Transformer reranker** | Ranking-only lift | Reported new NDCG@20 |

The cell "transformer reranker on legacy 200-item pool" isolates the ranking model quality claim directly. If the lift disappears in that cell, the reported 4.5% is entirely a retrieval artifact. If it persists, you have evidence the ranker is genuinely stronger.

---

**2. The specific claim being made**
The infrastructure change (scoring capacity → larger candidate pool) is treated as a neutral background condition rather than a design variable with its own effect.

**Why that claim might be wrong**
A retriever returning 500 candidates is not the same retriever returning 200 candidates at higher recall — it is a retriever with a different operating point on the precision-recall curve. The 500-item pool likely has lower average precision (more noise) but higher recall. Whether a transformer reranker handles noisy candidate pools better than a gradient-boosted reranker is a separate hypothesis from "the transformer is a better ranking model." GBDTs degrade gracefully on noisy candidate pools because they apply independent pairwise or pointwise losses; transformers with listwise losses can be sensitive to candidate set quality and may exhibit different behavior when noise increases.

**Evidence required**
Plot NDCG@20 for both rerankers as a function of candidate pool size (100, 200, 300, 400, 500) using the same retriever. This reveals whether the transformer's advantage is monotone in pool size (which would suggest pool-size sensitivity) or flat (which would suggest a genuine ranking signal advantage).

---

### Group B — Replay Evaluation Introduces a Directional Bias Against the New System's Retriever, But Also Masks Its True Recall

**3. The specific claim being made**
Replay evaluation on impression logs provides an unbiased estimate of ranking quality.

**Why that claim might be wrong**
Replay evaluation only credits items that appeared in the historical impression logs. Items the new retriever returns that were *never* shown to users receive zero relevance signal — they are invisible to the metric even if users would have preferred them. This creates a systematic undercount of the new retriever's benefit: items outside the legacy impression distribution are graded as irrelevant by default, not because they are irrelevant but because the logging policy never exposed them. This is the classic missing-data problem in counterfactual evaluation.

The bias cuts in an interesting direction here: it partially penalizes the new retriever's novel candidates, which means the true system improvement may be *larger* than reported — but it also means the reported metric cannot be trusted as a clean signal of the system's actual user-facing quality.

**Evidence required**
Estimate the fraction of the new retriever's 500-item pools that fall outside the historical impression distribution. If this fraction is high (>30% of candidates have zero logged impressions), the replay metric is measuring mostly the overlap region between the two systems' retrieval outputs, not the new system's full capability. One approach: report NDCG@20 restricted only to items with ≥1 logged impression in the evaluation window, alongside item-level coverage rates for each candidate pool.

---

**4. The specific claim being made**
The 5% uniform user sample is representative, so the NDCG@20 estimate generalizes to the full eligible population.

**Why that claim might be wrong**
The 30-day log expiry constraint means the eligible population is already conditioned on recency — only users with activity in the last 30 days are eligible. This selects for active, engaged users and excludes lapsed or low-frequency users. Uniform sampling from this already-filtered population does not recover the full traffic distribution. If the new two-tower model was designed to improve long-tail or cold-start users (a common motivation for switching from matrix factorization), those users may be underrepresented in the eligible pool due to sparse interaction histories, and the metric improvement among the active cohort may not reflect the model's behavior on the segment that motivated the architectural change.

**Evidence required**
Stratify NDCG@20 by user engagement tier (high, medium, low interaction frequency in the 30-day window). If the lift is concentrated in the high-engagement tier, the active-user bias is material. If it is uniform or stronger in the low-engagement tier, the sampling concern is less critical.

---

### Group C — Position Bias in Impression Logs Is Unaddressed

**5. The specific claim being made**
Observed clicks in the impression logs reflect true user preferences, making them valid relevance labels for NDCG computation.

**Why that claim might be wrong**
Impression logs carry heavy position bias: items shown at rank 1 receive clicks at substantially higher rates than items shown at rank 5, independent of true quality. If the legacy system's logs are used as relevance labels without inverse propensity score (IPS) correction, the relevance signal encodes the legacy system's own ranking decisions. Items the legacy ranker ranked highly will appear more relevant because they were clicked more often — because they were shown first. This means the evaluation implicitly rewards systems that agree with the legacy ranker's ranking decisions, not systems that surface genuinely preferred items.

**Why this is particularly sharp here**
The new system uses a listwise transformer reranker with a fundamentally different ranking function. It may surface a different ordering than the legacy GBDT. Items it ranks highly that the legacy system ranked lower will appear less "relevant" in the impression log simply because they received fewer exposures. The metric thus contains a systematic disadvantage for the new ranker, and the 4.5% gain would be observed *despite* this headwind — which is interesting — but it cannot be interpreted as a calibrated estimate of true quality improvement without IPS correction.

**Evidence required**
Apply propensity-weighted relevance labels (e.g., position-based model correction) to the click data before computing NDCG@20. The corrected and uncorrected NDCG numbers should be reported together. If they diverge substantially, the raw metric is not reliable for this comparison.

---

### Group D — The Claim Leadership Wants to Make Is Not Supported Even If Everything Else Were Fixed

**6. The specific claim being made**
The 4.5% offline NDCG@20 lift establishes that the new *ranking model* is stronger, and this justifies prioritizing the new stack.

**Why that claim might be wrong**
Even setting aside Groups A–C, the experiment measures the *system* gain, not the ranking model gain. The two-tower retrieval model is itself a ranking model operating at the retrieval stage; it produces a score distribution over the item space that shapes which items are even evaluable by the reranker. The GBDT reranker and transformer reranker are not the only things that changed — the retrieval model, the candidate pool size, the retrieval score distribution fed to the reranker as a feature (if used), and the infrastructure scoring capacity all changed simultaneously.

Leadership's framing — "the new ranking model is stronger" — implies the transformer reranker is responsible for the lift and therefore should be the focus of investment, iteration, and credit. The experiment cannot support this framing. It supports only the claim: "the new end-to-end stack produced higher NDCG@20 than the legacy end-to-end stack on this evaluation protocol, conditional on all the biases described above."

**Evidence required**
The 2×2 factorial in Issue 1 is necessary but not sufficient. The team should also run the transformer reranker on top of the legacy two-tower retriever (if accessible) and vice versa, to confirm the interaction term is small relative to the main effects. If there is a strong interaction (the transformer reranker is only better with the new retriever's candidate distribution), that finding itself is important product knowledge — it means the components are coupled and cannot be maintained or iterated independently.

---

### Summary: What Analysis Is Required Before Making the Leadership Claim

| Analysis | What it resolves |
|---|---|
| 2×2 factorial: {legacy, new} retriever × {GBDT, transformer} reranker | Separates retrieval lift from ranking lift |
| NDCG@20 vs. candidate pool size sweep | Tests whether transformer advantage is pool-size-dependent |
| Impression coverage rate for new retriever's novel candidates | Quantifies replay evaluation's missing-data bias |
| Stratified NDCG@20 by user engagement tier | Tests whether active-user selection bias distorts the estimate |
| IPS-corrected NDCG@20 | Removes position bias from relevance labels |

The 4.5% lift is a real signal that something improved, but the experiment as designed cannot tell leadership *what* improved, *why*, or whether the improvement is attributable to the ranking model they are being asked to endorse. Before the offline result is cited as evidence that the new ranker is stronger, at minimum the 2×2 factorial must be run — that is the single analysis that directly tests the claim being made.
defender_raw:
`★ Insight ─────────────────────────────────────`
This scenario is a textbook example of **confounded offline evaluation** — a situation where multiple design changes interact with the measurement protocol itself, making causal attribution impossible without additional factorial structure. The NDCG metric is sound; it's the experimental scaffold around it that breaks attribution.
`─────────────────────────────────────────────────`

---

## Benchmark Design Assessment

### The Central Problem: Two Changes, One Number

The reported 4.5% relative NDCG@20 gain conflates at least three distinct sources of lift, with no way to decompose them from the current design:

| Source | What changed | Can the current experiment isolate it? |
|---|---|---|
| Retrieval quality | Matrix factorization → two-tower | No |
| Ranking quality | GBM → listwise transformer | No |
| Retrieval budget | 200 candidates → 500 candidates | No |
| Their interaction | New reranker × new candidate pool | No |

Product leadership wants to claim **the new ranking model is stronger**. The experiment cannot support that claim as written.

---

### Flaw 1 — Asymmetric Candidate Pools (Most Critical)

The evaluation scores each reranker only on its own retriever's output. This is the most structurally damaging problem.

NDCG@20 measures how well a reranker orders the items it sees. If the two-tower retriever's 500-item pool contains systematically more relevant items than the legacy 200-item pool (which is the entire point of building a better retriever), the transformer reranker begins from a higher-quality starting position *before it does any ranking work*. The reported gain is then a property of the **input set**, not the ranker.

A concrete illustration: suppose both rankers are equally skilled, but the two-tower's wider funnel surfaces 30 additional items that users would actually click. The transformer's NDCG@20 rises simply because those items are eligible to be ranked into the top 20 — the legacy ranker never had the opportunity to place them there.

This means the experiment measures:

```
(two-tower retriever + transformer reranker + 500-item budget)
        vs.
(MF retriever + GBM reranker + 200-item budget)
```

and calls the delta a "ranking model improvement."

---

### Flaw 2 — Replay Evaluation Amplifies Retrieval Scope Bias

Replay evaluation scores items based on observed user interactions in historical logs. Items that were never shown to users have no ground-truth relevance label.

Because the legacy retriever operated at 200 candidates, **most of the 300 additional candidates the two-tower returns have no replay label** — they were never impressed. Standard replay practice drops or ignores unlabeled items, which means the two-tower's 500-item pool, after label filtering, may shrink to something close to the legacy pool in effective coverage. But if it doesn't — if the two-tower retrieves more previously-impressed items, perhaps because of better semantic alignment — the replay scoring inherits that structural advantage.

The team should report: after intersection with labeled impressions, what is the effective labeled candidate pool size for each system? If those sizes differ materially, the evaluation is not comparable.

---

### Flaw 3 — No Factorial Ablation

The correct experimental design for this question is a 2×2:

| | Legacy retriever (200) | Two-tower retriever (500) |
|---|---|---|
| **GBM reranker** | Baseline | Retrieval-only gain |
| **Transformer reranker** | Ranking-only gain | Full stack |

The team skipped this because both changes were deployed together in code review. That is an engineering workflow problem, not a justification for an incomplete experiment. The ablation does not require re-deploying legacy infrastructure — it can be run offline by:

1. Running the two-tower retriever and scoring it with the legacy GBM reranker (cross-system condition).
2. Running the legacy retriever and scoring it with the transformer reranker (cross-system condition).

Both conditions can be evaluated on the same replay dataset the team already has. The only prerequisite is that both rerankers must score a common candidate set — which is achievable post-hoc.

---

### Flaw 4 — Budget Confound Is Not a Model Quality Claim

The new infrastructure can score more items. That is a latency/cost engineering win. It is not evidence that the new ranking model is stronger — it is evidence that the team can afford to run more candidates. If the legacy GBM were given 500 candidates instead of 200, some fraction of the observed lift would likely persist without any model change.

To separate "model quality" from "retrieval budget," the minimum credible test is:

- Fix the retriever (use either one) and vary only the reranker.
- Fix the candidate count at both 200 and 500, and vary only the reranker.

Neither test exists in the current experiment.

---

### Flaw 5 — Replay Evaluation Has Known Ceiling Biases (Secondary, but Worth Noting)

Replay evaluation from impression logs carries structural optimism for systems that are more similar to the logging policy (i.e., the legacy system). A new two-tower retriever that surfaces different items may be penalized by the replay evaluator simply because those items were never shown — not because they are bad recommendations. This is the classic **off-policy evaluation** problem.

This cuts *against* the new system in replay, meaning the true advantage could be larger. But it also means the NDCG numbers are not clean estimates of real-world utility for either system. The direction of this bias is uncertain without knowing how much overlap exists between each retriever's output and the impression log.

---

### Flaw 6 — Sample Representativeness Is Underspecified

5% of users, uniformly sampled, sounds reasonable — but "all traffic segments" may include heavy users, casual users, new users, and content-type segments with very different behavioral signatures. The claim that offline NDCG generalizes to the full population requires:

- Verifying that the 5% sample's behavioral distribution (session length, click-through rate, content diversity) matches the full population.
- Checking that the leave-last-week-out split does not systematically favor either system (e.g., if the last week had unusual content supply or demand).

These are softer concerns compared to the confound issues above, but they matter for the strength of the claim.

---

## What Additional Analysis Is Required

Listed in priority order:

**1. (Blocking) Run the cross-system ablation offline.**
Score the two-tower candidates with the GBM reranker and the legacy candidates with the transformer reranker. This decomposes the 4.5% into retrieval lift, ranking lift, and interaction. Without this, the team cannot make any specific claim about which model component is responsible.

**2. (Blocking) Equalize candidate pool sizes and re-run.**
Re-run the evaluation with both rerankers receiving the same 200-item and 500-item pools. This separates the budget effect from the model effect.

**3. (Recommended) Report effective labeled pool sizes.**
For each system, report how many of the retrieved candidates have replay labels. If the two-tower's 500-item pool has 180 labeled items and the legacy's 200-item pool has 190, the evaluation is effectively comparable. If the two-tower has 350 labeled items, it has a structural scoring advantage.

**4. (Recommended) Sensitivity check on the last-week split.**
Re-run evaluation on weeks N-2 and N-3 to check whether the lift is stable across weeks. A single-week hold-out can be misleading if that week had unusual catalog dynamics.

---

## What the Team Can and Cannot Claim

| Claim | Supported by current experiment? |
|---|---|
| "The full new stack outperforms the full legacy stack on replay NDCG@20" | Yes, narrowly — but only on this specific evaluation setup |
| "The new ranking model is stronger" | **No** — retrieval quality and budget are confounded |
| "The two-tower retriever improves candidate quality" | Partially — but replay coverage limits attribution |
| "The new stack will improve user engagement in production" | Unknown — replay NDCG is not a direct proxy for engagement without calibration |

`★ Insight ─────────────────────────────────────`
**Why confounded offline experiments are dangerous at product review gates:** A 4.5% relative NDCG lift is large enough to be compelling to stakeholders but small enough that it could be entirely explained by the retrieval budget increase alone. The factorial ablation is not academic rigor for its own sake — it prevents launching a computationally expensive transformer reranker that may add no marginal quality above what a cheaper GBM would achieve given the same candidate pool. The cost of the ablation (one offline scoring run) is far lower than the cost of a misinformed architecture decision.
`─────────────────────────────────────────────────`

---

**Bottom line for product leadership:** The experiment demonstrates that the new full stack produces better offline rankings than the legacy full stack under this specific protocol. It does not, and cannot, support the claim that the new ranking model is the source of that improvement. Before attributing the lift to the transformer reranker, the team needs the 2×2 ablation. That ablation is feasible on the data they already have and should be a gate condition before using this result to justify the ranking architecture decision.
adjudication_raw: Both critic and defender produce substantive, well-structured analysis. The critic identifies five core issues: (1) different candidate pools invalidating the primary ranker comparison, (2) the 500-vs-200 candidate count creating a structural NDCG advantage, (3) replay evaluation's missing-data bias, (4) position bias in impression logs, and (5) the leadership claim outrunning the experimental evidence. The defender concedes all structural flaws: it explicitly states the experiment conflates retrieval quality, ranking quality, and retrieval budget; that the 2x2 factorial ablation is necessary; and that the claim "the new ranking model is stronger" cannot be supported. The defender argues only that the full-stack comparison result is real in the narrow replay-evaluation sense. This concession aligns with the critique. The verdict is critique_wins because the specific claim product leadership wants to make — that the new ranking model is stronger — is affirmatively conceded by the defender to be unsupported. An empirical test is required to decompose the gain.
all_issues_raised: Issue 1: Candidate pool confound invalidates ranker comparison (different pools, not same task)
Issue 2: 500-vs-200 candidate count creates structural NDCG advantage independent of ranking quality
Issue 3: Replay evaluation has missing-data bias against new retriever novel candidates
Issue 4: Position bias in impression logs disadvantages new ranker
Issue 5: User sampling active-user bias may underrepresent cold-start users
Issue 6: Leadership claim (ranking model is stronger) exceeds what experiment establishes

empirical_test.condition: 2x2 factorial: {legacy MF, two-tower} retriever × {GBM, transformer} reranker, evaluated on frozen common candidate sets at both 200- and 500-item depths
empirical_test.supports_critique_if: On a frozen common 500-item candidate pool, the transformer reranker loses the reported 4.5% relative NDCG gain, or gain is fully explained by retrieval recall differences when both rerankers see the same candidates
empirical_test.supports_defense_if: With both rerankers evaluated on the same frozen candidate pool at the same depth, the transformer reranker still improves NDCG@20 by at least 2% relative, and the new retriever also improves recall separately
empirical_test.ambiguous_if: The common-pool reranker gain is positive but under 2% relative while the new retriever contributes most of the stack-level improvement
